_cite.reason: URL-normalization 1c: strip trailing markdown-emphasis `*`/backtick glued to citation URLs in intelligence_item_sections.content_md (customer-checkable link + validate_item_provenance criterion-2 exact-compare). Operator ruling 2026-07-04. Same transform as the write-site fix.

prior.content_md:
The IMO 2023 GHG Strategy and its implementing instruments do not create direct registration or producer-responsibility obligations on freight forwarders. Regulatory registration obligations attach to **shipowners, operators, and flag states** under MARPOL Annex VI frameworks. However, the workspace faces indirect data-collection and reporting obligations through two channels:

**IMO Data Collection System (DCS) — indirect data access obligations**: The DCS requires verified fuel oil consumption data for ships ≥5,000 GT to be reported annually by ship operators to flag administrations and then to IMO's GISIS database. This data is the primary input for CII ratings and, under the future NZF, for GFI attainment records. The workspace is not a reporting party but must collect vessel-level DCS data from carriers to populate its own clients' Scope 3 disclosure requirements. Data fields the workspace must collect from carriers per voyage: IMO vessel number, fuel type(s) consumed (including biofuel blend percentages), voyage distance (nautical miles), cargo mass carried (metric tonnes), and the vessel's most recent verified annual CII rating. These fields are not yet standardised in commercial invoicing formats, creating a gap between regulatory reporting (flag-state/IMO-facing) and commercial data-sharing (carrier-to-forwarder).

**Scope 3 / ESG disclosure — published vs. promised**: Several reporting frameworks that apply to the workspace's clients (EU CSRD / ESRS E1, ISSB IFRS S2, GHG Protocol Corporate Value Chain Standard) require Scope 3 Category 4 (upstream transportation) and Category 9 (downstream transportation) data from their logistics providers. As of the date of this brief, no uniform IMO-mandated mechanism exists for carriers to pass per-shipment well-to-wake emission data to cargo interests; this is a **promised but not yet released** element of the NZF implementation guidelines under development at MEPC 84 and ISWG-GHG 21. The workspace should not wait for this mechanism; it should proactively negotiate data-sharing provisions into carrier service contracts now, specifying WTW GHG intensity per voyage leg using LCA Guideline (MEPC.376(80)) methodology.

**NZF fuel certification — emerging requirement**: Once adopted, the NZF will require fuels to be verified through an IMO-recognised Sustainable Fuel Certification Scheme. The workspace does not certify fuels but should confirm that carriers using alternative fuels on nominated trade lanes (Americas–Europe air-ocean intermodal connections; transatlantic and transpacific ocean lanes) hold valid certification for any fuel represented as zero or near-zero, as this affects both the vessel's GFI attainment and the validity of any green shipping claim the workspace makes to clients.

*Source: IMO — 2023 IMO Strategy on Reduction of GHG Emissions from Ships, IMO, 2023. https://www.imo.org/en/ourwork/environment/pages/2023-imo-strategy-on-reduction-of-ghg-emissions-from-ships.aspx; IMO Net-Zero Framework FAQs, IMO, 2025. https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/hottopics/pages/faqs-the-imo-net-zero-framework.aspx*
